title: Southern Hemisphere

infobox.value: is the half ([hemisphere](/wiki/Hemispheres_of_Earth "Hemispheres of Earth")) of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") that is [south](/wiki/South "South") of the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"). It contains all or part of five [continents](/wiki/Continent "Continent")[[1]](#cite_note-1) (the whole of [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), the whole of [Australia](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)"), about 90% of [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), about one-third of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), and some islands off the continental [mainland](/wiki/Mainland "Mainland") of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia")) and four [oceans](/wiki/Ocean "Ocean") (the whole [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean"), the majority of the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"), the [South Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), and the [South Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean")), as well as [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") and most of the [Pacific Islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_in_the_Pacific_Ocean "List of islands in the Pacific Ocean") in [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania"). Its surface is 80.9% water, compared with 60.7% water in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere"), and it contains 32.7% of Earth's land.[[2]](#cite_note-2) More than 850 million people live in the Southern Hemisphere, representing around 10–12% of the total global human population.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Half of Earth that is south of the Equator

This article is about the southern half of planet Earth. For use of the term describing astronomical observations, see [Southern celestial hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_celestial_hemisphere "Southern celestial hemisphere").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Southern_Hemisphere_LamAz.png/250px-Southern_Hemisphere_LamAz.png)](/wiki/File:Southern_Hemisphere_LamAz.png)

The Southern Hemisphere from above the [South Pole](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Global_hemispheres.svg/250px-Global_hemispheres.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Global_hemispheres.svg)

The Southern Hemisphere is highlighted in yellow. The hemispheres appear to be unequal in this image because [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") is not shown.

The **Southern Hemisphere** is the half ([hemisphere](/wiki/Hemispheres_of_Earth "Hemispheres of Earth")) of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") that is [south](/wiki/South "South") of the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"). It contains all or part of five [continents](/wiki/Continent "Continent")[[1]](#cite_note-1) (the whole of [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), the whole of [Australia](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)"), about 90% of [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), about one-third of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), and some islands off the continental [mainland](/wiki/Mainland "Mainland") of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia")) and four [oceans](/wiki/Ocean "Ocean") (the whole [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean"), the majority of the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"), the [South Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), and the [South Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean")), as well as [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") and most of the [Pacific Islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_in_the_Pacific_Ocean "List of islands in the Pacific Ocean") in [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania"). Its surface is 80.9% water, compared with 60.7% water in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere"), and it contains 32.7% of Earth's land.[[2]](#cite_note-2) More than 850 million people live in the Southern Hemisphere, representing around 10–12% of the total global human population.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

Owing to the tilt of [Earth's rotation](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation") relative to the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") and the [ecliptic plane](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic"), [summer](/wiki/Summer "Summer") is from December to February (inclusive) and [winter](/wiki/Winter "Winter") is from June to August (inclusive). September 22 or 23 is the vernal [equinox](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox") and March 20 or 21 is the autumnal equinox. The [South Pole](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole") is in the centre of the southern hemispherical region.

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Hemisphere&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

Southern Hemisphere [climates](/wiki/Climate "Climate") tend to be slightly milder than those at similar latitudes in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere"), except in the [Antarctic](/wiki/Antarctic "Antarctic") which is colder than the [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic"). This is because the Southern Hemisphere has significantly more ocean and much less land; water heats up and cools down more slowly than land.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The differences are also attributed to oceanic heat transfer and differing extents of greenhouse trapping.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Aurora_australis_panorama.jpg/500px-Aurora_australis_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aurora_australis_panorama.jpg)

[Aurora australis](/wiki/Aurora_(astronomy) "Aurora (astronomy)") appearing in the night sky of [Swifts Creek](/wiki/Swifts_Creek "Swifts Creek"), 100 km (62 mi) north of [Lakes Entrance, Victoria](/wiki/Lakes_Entrance,_Victoria "Lakes Entrance, Victoria"), Australia

In the Southern Hemisphere, the Sun passes from east to west through the north, although north of the [Tropic of Capricorn](/wiki/Tropic_of_Capricorn "Tropic of Capricorn") the [mean Sun](/wiki/Solar_time#Mean_solar_time "Solar time") can be directly overhead or due south at midday. The Sun follows a right-to-left trajectory through the northern sky unlike the left-to-right motion of the Sun when seen from the Northern Hemisphere as it passes through the southern sky. Sun-cast shadows turn anticlockwise throughout the day and [sundials](/wiki/Sundial "Sundial") have the hours increasing in the [anticlockwise](/wiki/Clockwise "Clockwise") direction. During [solar eclipses](/wiki/Solar_eclipse "Solar eclipse") viewed from a point to the south of the Tropic of Capricorn, the Moon moves from left to right on the disc of the Sun (see, for example, photos with timings of the [solar eclipse of November 13, 2012](/wiki/Solar_eclipse_of_November_13,_2012 "Solar eclipse of November 13, 2012")), while viewed from a point to the north of the [Tropic of Cancer](/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer") (i.e., in the Northern Hemisphere), the Moon moves from right to left during solar eclipses.

The [Coriolis effect](/wiki/Coriolis_effect "Coriolis effect") causes cyclones and tropical storms to spin clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere, as opposed to [anticlockwise](/wiki/Anticlockwise "Anticlockwise") in the Northern Hemisphere.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The southern [temperate zone](/wiki/Temperateness "Temperateness"), a subsection of the Southern Hemisphere, is nearly all oceanic.

The [Sagittarius](/wiki/Sagittarius_(constellation) "Sagittarius (constellation)") constellation that includes the [galactic centre](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") is a southern constellation as well as both [Magellanic Clouds](/wiki/Magellanic_Clouds "Magellanic Clouds"). This, combined with clearer skies, makes for excellent viewing of the night sky from the Southern Hemisphere with brighter and more numerous stars.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/AuroraAustralisDisplay.jpg/250px-AuroraAustralisDisplay.jpg)](/wiki/File:AuroraAustralisDisplay.jpg)

Aurora australis appearing from [Stewart Island/Rakiura](/wiki/Stewart_Island "Stewart Island") in the south of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand")

Forests in the Southern Hemisphere have special features which set them apart from those in the Northern Hemisphere. Both [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") and [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") share, for example, unique [beech](/wiki/Beech "Beech") species or *[Nothofagus](/wiki/Nothofagus "Nothofagus")*, and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") has members of the closely related genera *[Lophozonia](/wiki/Lophozonia "Lophozonia")* and *[Fuscospora](/wiki/Fuscospora "Fuscospora")*. The [eucalyptus](/wiki/Eucalyptus "Eucalyptus") is native to [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") but is now also planted in [Southern Africa](/wiki/Southern_Africa "Southern Africa") and [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") for pulp production, and increasingly, [biofuel](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel") uses.

One of the most notable animals to be found almost exclusively in the Southern Hemisphere is the [penguin](/wiki/Penguin "Penguin"). A species is found around [Isabela Island](/wiki/Isabela_Island_(Gal%C3%A1pagos) "Isabela Island (Galápagos)") on the [Galápagos](/wiki/Gal%C3%A1pagos_Islands "Galápagos Islands") archipelago in the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"), which straddles the equator.[[8]](#cite_note-8) However, most of Isabela and the rest of the archipelago is located in the Southern Hemisphere, and it is deemed by the [International Hydrographic Organization](/wiki/International_Hydrographic_Organization "International Hydrographic Organization") as being wholly within the South Pacific Ocean, rather than the North Pacific.[[9]](#cite_note-IHO1953-9)

## Demographics and human geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Hemisphere&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Demographics and human geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Apollo17WorldReversed.jpg/250px-Apollo17WorldReversed.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apollo17WorldReversed.jpg)

A photo of Earth from [Apollo 17](/wiki/Apollo_17 "Apollo 17") (*[Blue Marble](/wiki/The_Blue_Marble "The Blue Marble")*) with the South Pole at the top and the continent of Africa

More than 850 million people live in the Southern Hemisphere, representing around 10–12% of the total global human population.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) Of those 850 million people, more than 203 million live in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), the largest country by land area in the Southern Hemisphere, while more than 150 million live in [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), the most populous island in the world. The most populous country in the Southern Hemisphere is [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), with 275 million people (roughly 30 million of whom live north of the Equator on the northern portions of the islands of [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"), [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"), and [Sulawesi](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi"), as well as most of [North Maluku](/wiki/North_Maluku "North Maluku"), while the rest of the population lives in the Southern Hemisphere).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The most populous country entirely located within the Southern Hemisphere is [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania"), with an estimated population of roughly 67 million people as of 2024.[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") is one of the most spoken languages in the Southern Hemisphere, with over 230 million speakers in six countries – mostly in Brazil, but also in [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola"), [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique"), [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor"), and small parts of [São Tomé and Príncipe](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Tom%C3%A9_and_Pr%C3%ADncipe "São Tomé and Príncipe") that lie south of the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Among the largest metropolitan areas in the Southern Hemisphere are [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") (33 million people), [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") (22 million), [Kinshasa-Brazzaville](/wiki/Kinshasa-Brazzaville "Kinshasa-Brazzaville") (19 million), [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires") (16 million), [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") (12 million), [Lima](/wiki/Lima "Lima") (11 million), [Surabaya](/wiki/Surabaya_metropolitan_area "Surabaya metropolitan area") (10 million), [Luanda](/wiki/Luanda "Luanda"), [Bandung](/wiki/Bandung_metropolitan_area "Bandung metropolitan area") (9 million each), [Dar es Salaam](/wiki/Dar_es_Salaam "Dar es Salaam"), [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago "Santiago") (7 million each), [Johannesburg](/wiki/Johannesburg "Johannesburg"), [Semarang](/wiki/Semarang_metropolitan_area "Semarang metropolitan area"), [Belo Horizonte](/wiki/Belo_Horizonte "Belo Horizonte") (6 million each), [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") and [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town") (5 million each). Important financial and commercial centres in the Southern Hemisphere include [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), where the [B3 (stock exchange)](/wiki/B3_(stock_exchange) "B3 (stock exchange)") is headquartered, along with Sydney, home to the [Australian Securities Exchange](/wiki/Australian_Securities_Exchange "Australian Securities Exchange"), Jakarta, the seat of the [Indonesia Stock Exchange](/wiki/Indonesia_Stock_Exchange "Indonesia Stock Exchange"), [Johannesburg](/wiki/Johannesburg "Johannesburg"), home to the [Johannesburg Stock Exchange](/wiki/JSE_Limited "JSE Limited"), and Buenos Aires, headquarters of the [Buenos Aires Stock Exchange](/wiki/Buenos_Aires_Stock_Exchange "Buenos Aires Stock Exchange"), the oldest stock market in the Southern Hemisphere.

Common tourist destinations in the Southern Hemisphere include [Bali](/wiki/Bali "Bali"), [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"), [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town"), [Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island"), [Lima](/wiki/Lima "Lima"), [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") and [Tahiti](/wiki/Tahiti "Tahiti").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) According to a 2017 report, the most popular Southern Hemisphere "[bucket list](/wiki/Kick_the_bucket "Kick the bucket")" destinations among Australians were [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), the [Galápagos Islands](/wiki/Gal%C3%A1pagos_Islands "Galápagos Islands"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[Quito, Ecuador](/wiki/Quito,_Ecuador "Quito, Ecuador") is the closest major city to the [equatorial line](/wiki/Equator "Equator") on the planet, and [Ushuaia, Argentina](/wiki/Ushuaia,_Argentina "Ushuaia, Argentina") claims the title of world's southernmost city. Cape Town, [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch"), [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart"), [Punta Arenas](/wiki/Punta_Arenas "Punta Arenas") and Ushuaia are officially acknowledged as the five international [Antarctic gateway cities](/wiki/Antarctic_gateway_cities "Antarctic gateway cities") that serve as primary entry points for travel to the Antarctic region.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Among the most developed nations in the Southern Hemisphere is Australia, with a nominal GDP per capita of US$63,487 and a [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (HDI) of 0.946, the tenth-highest in the world as of the 2024 report. New Zealand is also well developed, with a nominal GDP per capita of US$48,072 and an HDI of 0.939, putting it at number 16 in the world in 2024. The least developed nations in the Southern Hemisphere cluster in Africa and Oceania, with [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique") and [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi") at the lowest ends of the HDI, at 0.461 (number 183 in the world) and 0.420 (number 187 in the world), respectively. The nominal GDPs per capita of these two countries do not go above US$650, a tiny fraction of the incomes enjoyed by Australians and New Zealanders.

The Southern Hemisphere has long been secondary in the global distribution of demographic, economic and political power, as it has less land than the Northern Hemisphere.[[17]](#cite_note-hempower-17) In recent times, however, countries such as Australia have made greater efforts to economically engage with those from their hemisphere.[[17]](#cite_note-hempower-17) Before the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"), the Southern Hemisphere was largely cut off from the cultural constructs of the [Western](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") and [Eastern worlds](/wiki/Eastern_world "Eastern world").[[17]](#cite_note-hempower-17) Some view both the West and the East as being Northern Hemisphere-centric concepts.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The most widespread religions in the modern Southern Hemisphere are [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), prevalent in South America, Africa, Oceania, and [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor"), followed by [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") in East Africa and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), which is mostly concentrated on/around the islands of [Bali](/wiki/Bali "Bali"), [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius"), and [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The oldest continuously inhabited city in the Southern Hemisphere is [Bogor](/wiki/Bogor "Bogor"), in western [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), which was founded in 669. Ancient texts from the Hindu kingdoms prevalent in the area definitively record 669 CE as the year when Bogor was founded. However, some evidence shows that [Zanzibar](/wiki/Zanzibar_City "Zanzibar City"), an ancient port with around 200,000 inhabitants off the coast of [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania"), may be older than Bogor. A Greco-Roman text written between 1 and 100 CE, the [*Periplus of the Erythraean Sea*](/wiki/Periplus_of_the_Erythrean_Sea "Periplus of the Erythrean Sea"), mentioned the island of *Menuthias* ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek"): Μενουθιάς) as a trading port on the east African coast, which is probably the small Tanzanian island of [Unguja](/wiki/Unguja "Unguja") on which Zanzibar is located. The oldest monumental civilizations in the Southern Hemisphere are the [Norte Chico civilization](/wiki/Norte_Chico_civilization "Norte Chico civilization") and [Casma–Sechin culture](/wiki/Casma%E2%80%93Sechin_culture "Casma–Sechin culture") from the northern coast of Peru. These civilizations built cities, pyramids, and plazas in the coastal river valleys of northern Peru with some ruins dating back to 3600 BCE. [Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island"), located about 3,500 km (2,200 mi) from [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") and [French Polynesia](/wiki/French_Polynesia "French Polynesia"), is considered to be the most remote place on Earth to have been permanently inhabited by humans before the Age of Discovery.[[20]](#cite_note-20) It was settled by a [Polynesian](/wiki/Polynesians "Polynesians") group known as the [Rapa Nui](/wiki/Rapa_Nui_people "Rapa Nui people"). Areas of the Southern Hemisphere that had no contact with humans before the Age of Discovery include [Christmas Island](/wiki/Christmas_Island "Christmas Island") and [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius") (in the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean")), the [Galápagos Islands](/wiki/Gal%C3%A1pagos_Islands "Galápagos Islands"), [Juan Fernández Islands](/wiki/Juan_Fern%C3%A1ndez_Islands "Juan Fernández Islands") and [Lord Howe Island](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island "Lord Howe Island") (in the South Pacific), the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands") and [Tristan da Cunha](/wiki/Tristan_da_Cunha "Tristan da Cunha") (in the [South Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean")) and the continent of [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica").

## Continents or submerged continents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Hemisphere&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Continents or submerged continents")]

[Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa")
:   About one-third of the continent, from south of [Mogadishu](/wiki/Mogadishu "Mogadishu") in [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia") in the east to south of [Libreville](/wiki/Libreville "Libreville") in [Gabon](/wiki/Gabon "Gabon") in the west. From the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 0°) to [Cape Agulhas](/wiki/Cape_Agulhas "Cape Agulhas") ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 34°50′S).

[Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica")
:   The entire continent and its associated islands are within the Southern Hemisphere. From [Prime Head](/wiki/Prime_Head "Prime Head"), at the northern tip of the [Trinity Peninsula](/wiki/Trinity_Peninsula "Trinity Peninsula") ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 63°12′48″S) to the [South Pole](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole") ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 90° S).

[Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia")
:   The entire continental mainland is within the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere"); only the southern portion of [Maritime Southeast Asia](/wiki/Maritime_Southeast_Asia "Maritime Southeast Asia"), including [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor") and most of [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), plus the [British Indian Ocean Territory](/wiki/British_Indian_Ocean_Territory "British Indian Ocean Territory") and [two out of 26 atolls](/wiki/Atolls_of_the_Maldives "Atolls of the Maldives") of the Maldives, part of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") in the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"), are in the Southern Hemisphere. From the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 0°) to [Pamana Island](/wiki/Pamana_Island "Pamana Island"), Indonesia ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 11°00'S).

[Australia](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)")
:   The entire continent and most of its associated islands are within the Southern Hemisphere. From the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 0°) to [Bishop and Clerk Islets](/wiki/Bishop_and_Clerk_Islets "Bishop and Clerk Islets"), Tasmania, Australia ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 55°03′ S).

[South America](/wiki/South_America "South America")
:   Most of the continent, from south of the [Amazon River](/wiki/Amazon_River "Amazon River") mouth in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") in the east to north of [Quito](/wiki/Quito "Quito") in [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador") in the west. From the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 0°) to [Águila Islet](/wiki/%C3%81guila_Islet "Águila Islet"), Diego Ramírez Islands, Chile ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 56°32′16″S), or, if the [South Sandwich Islands](/wiki/South_Sandwich_Islands "South Sandwich Islands") are included as part of South America, [Cook Island, South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands](/wiki/Cook_Island,_South_Sandwich_Islands "Cook Island, South Sandwich Islands") ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 59°29′20″S).

[Zealandia](/wiki/Zealandia "Zealandia")
:   The entire [submerged continent](/wiki/Submerged_continent "Submerged continent"), including [New Caledonia](/wiki/New_Caledonia "New Caledonia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island"), and other associated low-lying islands above sea level, is within the Southern Hemisphere. From [Belep](/wiki/Belep "Belep"), New Caledonia, France ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 19°45′00″S) to [Jacquemart Island](/wiki/Jacquemart_Island "Jacquemart Island") ([Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"): 52°37′S).
